sentences.text: Mm-hmm.
No.
I was alone.
And they took us away.
They said that we're going to work.
And they're going to bring us back home in the evening, which they didn't.
They put us on trucks that waiting for us.
I-- I couldn't tell you exact the amount.
But the trucks were loaded, mostly with young boys, young men, who they took away.
Yes.
They didn't touch any of the Gentiles.
Yes, but we didn't.
And nobody did, because they shipped us away like in a place that you wouldn't know how to get back home.
It was like empty fields, far away.
We were riding for quite a while.
Hours.
Then other trucks came after them, loaded with equipment, like big sledgehammers, picks, shovels.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had to unload those, pile them up just on the ground.
And when all the trucks left, some civilian Germans came by.
And they marked off on the fields where to put in those big, huge, wooden piles into the ground.
The hammer must have weighed more than I did at that time-- very, very heavy hammers.
And we had to beat those wooden spikes like into the ground.
That was the foundation for the barracks.
We started to build wooden barracks.
No.
No.
We had no idea.
So we knew that we were going to be there to build those barracks.
And they said, as so soon as we're going to be finished with it, we're all going to go home.
But it went on like this forever.
Nobody ever went home.
Unless--
Unless somebody got-- knocked off a leg or an arm, they would ship them home.
Yes.
There were the-- the guards in the brown uniforms.
They call them SA.
SA.
SS had black uniforms.
SA had the brown uniforms-- which you saw maybe Hitler from the beginning.
He would wear a brown uniform.
Than the Hitler Yugen, they called it, the younger kids, the younger boys, they wore all those brown uniforms.
With the swastikas.
The-- the older guards-- they had weapons.
The younger-- the kids, no.
For quite a while, because each time we finished the barracks in one place, like started off at Gogolin-- they shipped us to the next place.
They named it Ottmuth.
We start to do the same thing all over again.
Then when the barracks were finished, they would bring in more people-- older people, strong people, to start building [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] either factories, roads, or the autobahns in some places.
It was a whole new system for them.
That kept on for as long as I can remember.
At this time, it was almost three years.
Three years.
Let's just back up if we could, just for a little bit.
When you were first taken from home, when you thought you were coming back that night, how long were you in the first place you were?
Close to a year maybe.
You mean the camp?
That was the town.
It was Gogolin.
The camps-- that's what they used to call it-- Camp Gogolin.
Or Camp Ottmuth.
And so Ottmuth was the first one that you were at, and so that's the one you were at for almost--
I couldn't tell the exact time.
We had no way of knowing what day it was or what's going on.
We had nothing to go by.
We didn't know what time it was.
We just knew when the whistle blew in the morning, you had to get up.
When the whistle blew at night, you had to be already inside and lay down in the bunks.
It must have been, I think, eight or 10 hours, because we had to walk quite a while to work.
Terrible, because we had to wear our own clothes, which we-- nobody took anything with it, because they said you're going to be-- you're going to be back home in the evening.
So that's all you had on, is what you had on.
There was no way of washing your face even, or taking a shower or anything.
In my own clothes, in all those camps, I did-- until we got into the concentration camp.
When we got into the concentration camp, we had to-- when we walked through the main gate, we had to line up.
And they asked everybody to take their clothes off completely, and drop their pants in the ground to search you.
After that, they took you into a big washroom, like.
And there-- and there were buckets of-- big buckets-- of soap.
You had to go in and take a handful of soap to put on your body and wash yourself up.
After you got through with this, you had to stand in a line to collect the uniform.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the first uniform that they gave us.
Three.
Ottmuth, Gogolin, and Flossenburg.
OK.
Flossenburg is a camp, of concentration camp.
Yes.
Yes.
Graditz.
Yes.
No.
Only when they brought in some more people from my town later on.
Through asking them what's going on with the families, the city.
My parents were killed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that was the end of that.
Same with it.
Nobody from my family was alive.
I didn't ask.
I didn't want to ask because I knew there was only one way.
They got shot, because that's all they did.
They would line them up against a wall, or they would put them near a ditch.
From the beginning, it was a terrible camp.
It was very filthy, very dirty.
That was Flossenburg.
There must have been some war prisoners in there before us.
What we heard was that the Germans had a lot of the Russian [NON-ENGLISH], you know what they call them?
You know, they look like Chinese.
You know, there is a province of Russia, I think, where they all look like-- with the small eyes.
You know, like-- like Eskimos or something.
You know, something-- it's a different tribe.
And what Russia did, is they were the first ones, when the war started with Russia, I think, they send them to the front lines.
But instead of fighting, they gave up.
So they took by the hundreds of thousands of them, and they brought them into camps.
And they put them into camps.
So they put them in those big camps.
And then after they shipped them out to another camp, they brought us in.
At that time, by train.
The train ride was not that long.
It was like maybe a day, with German guards already on each wagon with guns, because the train was open on top.
So they were watching us.
On each end of the car, so nobody should be able to escape.
Not that I recall.
Not that I recall.
Not at that transport.
But then when they start putting us to work into different factories, into different roads, and so on-- at that time, it became already a little tough.
A lot of people didn't care anymore.
A lot of people tried to escape from work.
When we were working outside, especially building, at that time I remember I was throwing bricks all day.

And the guys, the builders, that were on top on the first or second floor, I had to-- they made us like-- out of a board, with two sticks hanging over the shoulders, so it should be able to rest on your shoulders.
And a little shelf on the bottom in the back, they kept piling up bricks on it, right up to the top.
And you had to climb up on this wooden ladder to deliver those bricks for them.
After that, they thought that was too slow, so they made us throw the bricks, you know, like from down the ground to the first floor.
From the first floor, another one would catch it and throw it to the second and third floor.
And our hands became so bad, that it looked like raw meat is hanging down from the bricks thrown.
We had no gloves or anything.
So the roughness of the stone would just eat into the fingers.
That was in Flossenburg.
And we did it like this in most all the camps, too.
That was I think in '43.
After we finished that, they shipped us to another camp.
And we had to do the same thing all over again.
At the prison camps, everybody was all right.
I mean, nobody would be better than the-- than the next, except the kapo.
You know what a kapo is?
You know, he was the one who had all the say, to line us up, to be responsible for us.
He would hit us even sometimes during the day if you didn't do the work right or fast enough.
With a stick, or a lot of them had like a rubber-- with the-- a big, you know, a rubber stick with the cable inside.
If he hit with this, it hurted terrible.
No.
I worked hard.
They had privileges.
They didn't sleep with us in the bunks in the room.
He had his own room.
He would have the kind of food like the German soldiers had.
He didn't have to wear the uniform at that time.
He had leather boots on, like the Germans.
But he did wear the band.
Or he-- I mean, he was actually like a German guard.
Like any other concentration camp.
Barracks, soldiers, no crematorium.
No, I just knew that I'm-- I just knew that that's going to be my life, that there's never going to be anything else, except it's going to be the rest of our lives.
We never thought of being ever released or liberated, because we didn't have any paper.
We didn't know any news.
We didn't know what time it was.
When we were not working, we were able to go outside the barracks, move around outside, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] or make up something-- games inside.
We would take pieces of paper-- if you found pieces of paper-- and make cards out of it, make believe that you are playing cards.
Nothing else.
If you were especially hard-working, and the German guard would like you, he would go into the camp.
He would report to his head above him, and once in a while they would give you some coupon-like, to be able to go in and get a couple cigarettes or some other things.
They had a place-- a little tiny place-- where they would take the coupons for cigarettes.
That was it.
You just lived from day to day like this.
In the morning, the whistle would blow at 5 o'clock.
Everybody had to jump off from the bunks, get dressed fast, go outside, be counted.
Then they ship you over towards the kitchen window, with the little [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] tin cup.
And they gave us soup in the morning.
It felt like it was old dishwater from the Germans maybe, that they washed their dishes.
That was the breakfast-- with a half inch slice of bread.
They said at that time that was made out of sawdust.
That was the food to go out to work for the whole day.
In the evening, when we got back, again lined up, counted.
Once in a while, somebody would not to go back to the camp.
He would hide, either wherever he was working so he could escape, or whatever.
And if they counted us and if there was somebody missing, they would call up every 10th man.
Every 10th, call him out from the lines.
And either they would hang him, or otherwise we would have to wait, even if it took all night, until the dogs of the Germans or somebody brought him back, dead or alive.
Yes, a lot.
Especially in a camp by the name of Gross-Rosen.
That was the worst camp that I can remember.
I think about a year.
About a year.
And why were you moved from Flossenburg to Gross-Rosen?
I don't know.
Yes.
No, no, no.
They just picked a certain amount, whatever they needed.
How many, I don't know.
That was even worse than all the other camps so far that I went to.
When we walked into that camp, they had mud right up to your chest, all over.
It must have been something-- a new camp at that time-- that they just put up the barracks, and that was it.
And when the rain came or something, it was muddy, terribly muddy.
And again, everybody had to throw their clothes off.
While we were taking our clothes off, at that spot-- maybe about 30, 40 feet away-- there were a lot of barracks.
And at the front of the barracks, there were a lot of piles of baby shoes, baby clothes, baby toys laying around.
So we expected that they must have had here either a warehouse from other camps, that they shipped children's clothes-- that the children were killed already.
Or they brought in children there and killed them in that camp.
And the shoes with the clothes and everything else, they saved for their own children, the Germans.
Because at that time, it was too hard for them to find already clothes in the stores for them.
So that was the-- a terrible shock and a terrible sight to see those things.
Then after they checked us out, we had to march down.
And I remember it was a hill to march down from the place where we stayed to the barracks to get washed.
While we were doing this, we saw prisoners with huge pushcarts, like-- dead bodies on the pushcarts, going towards the crematorium to throw them in there.
That went on like this every day, all day long-- back and forth, back and forth they would go.
Mm-hmm.
Then I-- that was in the mud.
But you could see they struggled even to bring those wagons in or out.
And it went on like this every single day, every time whenever we went outside.
Whenever you looked out, you saw the same thing happen with no end, back and forth.
It was a terrible, terrible place.
It didn't matter, because at the time, you just didn't care if you live or die.
So it didn't matter.
You didn't ask questions, how or what, or when.
You know, the only time that you knew that somebody is dying for a cause was when the one guy didn't show up back to work, to the camp, and they picked out every 10th person to be hung up.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And more than that, I recall on a Sunday, on a Sunday, we didn't go out to work.

But what we had to do was go out to the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what would you call this-- it was like a place where you go-- not the bathroom, but they had made outside ditches, with a piece of, like, four by four to lean against it or sit on it.
So every Sunday, we would have to go out and clean out those ditches with buckets, bring them out, across straight to the field and empty them over there.
And one Sunday, we had to go during the day-- I don't remember if it was 1 or 2 o'clock, towards the kitchen window.
And they made-- you know, Sunday you got dinners, like 12-- I mean, 1 or or 2 o'clock, to collect that.
